[^19]: ${ }^{34}$ Note that if Assumption 3 does not hold (i.e., $w<0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2} \leq C$ ), then the optimal solution is $x_{\ell}^{*}=-1$ for all $\ell$. This is what we ruled out with Assumption 3, before Theorem 1.

comments.title: Comparative static claim in Footnote 16
comments.paragraph: It can be verified that, for every $\ell \in\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$, the ratio $x_{\ell} / x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing (decreasing) in $\beta$ for the case of strategic complements (substitutes): thus the intensity of the strategic interaction shapes the relative importance of different principal components.
comments.quote: It can be verified that, for every $\ell \in\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$, the ratio $x_{\ell} / x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing (decreasing) in $\beta$ for the case of strategic complements (substitutes): thus the intensity of the strategic interaction shapes the relative importance of different principal components.
comments.message:
Footnote 16 states that, for each $\ell$, the ratio $x_\ell/x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing in $\beta$ when $\beta>0$ and decreasing in $\beta$ when $\beta<0$. Using the expression from Theorem 1, $x_\ell^* = w\alpha_\ell/(\mu - w\alpha_\ell)$ with $\alpha_\ell=(1-\beta\lambda_\ell)^{-2}$ and $\mu$ determined by (6), one can look at the small‑budget limit $C\to 0$, where $\mu\to\infty$ and hence
\[
\frac{x_\ell^*}{x_{\ell+1}^*}\to \frac{\alpha_\ell}{\alpha_{\ell+1}} = \left(\frac{1-\beta\lambda_{\ell+1}}{1-\beta\lambda_\ell}\right)^2.
\]
Differentiating this limiting expression with respect to $\beta$ gives a strictly positive derivative for all admissible $\beta$, irrespective of the sign of $\beta$. Thus, at least for small budgets, the ratio $x_\ell^*/x_{\ell+1}^*$ is increasing in $\beta$ both for strategic complements and for strategic substitutes, contrary to the literal wording of the footnote.

This is a local, not a global, calculation, but by continuity it is hard to reconcile with the claim that the ratio is everywhere decreasing in $\beta$ when $\beta<0$. It would be helpful to double‑check this comparative static and either (i) reverse the direction for the substitutes case, or (ii) clarify that the intended statement is about monotonicity in the intensity $|\beta|$ rather than in $\beta$ itself, or else drop the claim.

comments.title: Limiting direction of intervention in Proposition 1
comments.paragraph:
If $\beta>0$ (the game features strategic complements), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the first principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
2b. If $\beta<0$ (the game features strategic substitutes), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the last principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{n}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
comments.quote:
If $\beta>0$ (the game features strategic complements), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the first principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
2b. If $\beta<0$ (the game features strategic substitutes), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the last principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{n}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
comments.message:
In Proposition 1(2a–b) you state that, as $C\to\infty$, the cosine similarity between the optimal intervention and the relevant principal component converges to 1, and you later write that $\boldsymbol{y}^*\to\sqrt{C}\,\boldsymbol{u}^1(\boldsymbol{G})$ (or $\sqrt{C}\,\boldsymbol{u}^n(\boldsymbol{G})$). 

Given Theorem 1 and the budget constraint, what can generally be shown under Assumptions 1–3 and Property A is that $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)^2\to 1$ when $\beta>0$ (and analogously for $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ when $\beta<0$). For $w>0$ we have $x_\ell^* = w\alpha_\ell/(\mu-w\alpha_\ell)\ge 0$ and $\underline{y}_\ell^* = \underline{\hat b}_\ell x_\ell^*$. As $C\to\infty$, the component $\underline{y}_1^*$ dominates the budget, with $(\underline{y}_1^*)^2\sim C$, so
\[
\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1) 
= \frac{\underline{y}_1^*}{\sqrt{C}}
\to \mathrm{sign}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1),
\]
and similarly with $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ in the substitutes case. Thus, for a fixed orientation of the eigenvectors, the limit of the cosine similarity is $\pm 1$, with the sign determined by $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1$ (or $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^n$). 

Since eigenvectors are only defined up to sign, one can always reorient $\boldsymbol{u}^1$ (or $\boldsymbol{u}^n$) so that the limiting cosine similarity is $+1$, and the economic content of the result is that the intervention concentrates on the corresponding one-dimensional eigenspace. It would nonetheless be helpful to make this explicit—e.g., by formulating Proposition 1 in terms of $|\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)|\to 1$ (and $|\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^n)|\to 1$), or by stating any normalization of eigenvectors and/or restriction on the status quo vector that guarantees $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1>0$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^n>0.

comments.title: Sign error in discussion of Proposition 2 (substitutes case)
comments.paragraph:
If the game has the strategic substitutes property, $\beta<0$, then for any $\epsilon>0$, if $C> \frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{n-1}}{\alpha_{n}-\alpha_{n-1}}\right)^{2}$, then...
...
If $\beta<0$, then the term $\alpha_{n-1} /\left(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_{n}\right)$ is large when the difference $\lambda_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}$, which we call the "bottom gap," is small.
comments.quote:
If the game has the strategic substitutes property, $\beta<0$, then for any $\epsilon>0$, if $C> \frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{n-1}}{\alpha_{n}-\alpha_{n-1}}\right)^{2}$, then...
...
If $\beta<0$, then the term $\alpha_{n-1} /\left(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_{n}\right)$ is large when the difference $\lambda_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}$, which we call the "bottom gap," is small.
comments.message: In the paragraph interpreting Proposition 2 for the substitutes case, the factor governing the bound on $C$ is described as $\alpha_{n-1}/(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_n)$, whereas in the formal statement of the proposition the factor is $\alpha_{n-1}/(\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1})$ inside a square. Since for $\beta<0$ one has $\alpha_n>\alpha_{n-1}$, the latter uses a positive denominator and matches the expression used in the proof, while the former has the opposite sign. Because the ratio is squared in the bound, this does not affect the actual condition on $C$, but it introduces an avoidable notational inconsistency. It would be helpful to align the discussion with the proposition (using $\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1}$) so that the "corresponding factor for bottom $\alpha$'s" is written with the same ordering throughout.

comments.title: "Maximizer" vs. "minimizer" for smallest eigenvalues
comments.paragraph:
Turning next to strategic substitutes, recall that the smallest two eigenvalues, $\lambda_{n}$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$, can be written as follows:
$$
\lambda_{n}=\min _{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j}, \quad \lambda_{n-1}=\min _{\substack{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1 \\ \boldsymbol{u} \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{n}=0}} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j} .
$$
Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second; these are uniquely determined under Assumption 2.
comments.quote:
Turning next to strategic substitutes, recall that the smallest two eigenvalues, $\lambda_{n}$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$, can be written as follows:
$$
\lambda_{n}=\min _{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j}, \quad \lambda_{n-1}=\min _{\substack{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1 \\ \boldsymbol{u} \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{n}=0}} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j} .
$$
Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second; these are uniquely determined under Assumption 2.
comments.message: In the substitutes discussion you correctly write the Rayleigh–Ritz characterizations for $\lambda_n$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$ as minimization problems, but then state that $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ and $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ are "maximizers" of these problems. Since the display uses $\min$ and the next sentence reintroduces $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ via an explicit $\arg\min$, these eigenvectors should be described as minimizers, not maximizers. This is clearly just a wording slip and does not affect any results, but it would be good to correct it for consistency with the complements case and with standard spectral terminology.

comments.title: Typo in Lagrangian in proof of Theorem 1
comments.paragraph:
Observe that the Lagrangian corresponding to the maximization problem is

$$
\mathcal{L}=w \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}\right)^{2} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}+\mu\left[C-\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} x_{\ell}^{2}\right] .
$$

Taking our observation above that the constraint is binding at $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, together with the standard results on the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, the first-order conditions must hold exactly at the optimum with a positive $\mu$ :
comments.quote:
Observe that the Lagrangian corresponding to the maximization problem is

$$
\mathcal{L}=w \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}\right)^{2} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}+\mu\left[C-\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} x_{\ell}^{2}\right] .
$$

Taking our observation above that the constraint is binding at $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, together with the standard results on the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, the first-order conditions must hold exactly at the optimum with a positive $\mu$ :
comments.message:
The displayed Lagrangian in the proof of Theorem 1 appears to omit a square on the status‑quo component. Just above, the problem in the $x_\ell$ variables is written as
$$
\max_x\; w\sum_{\ell=1}^n \alpha_\ell (1+x_\ell)^2 \underline{\hat b}_\ell^2
\quad \text{s.t. } \sum_{\ell=1}^n \underline{\hat b}_\ell^2 x_\ell^2 \le C,
$$
so the corresponding Lagrangian should involve $\underline{\hat b}_\ell^2$ in the first term as well. The first‑order condition you derive immediately afterward matches the derivative of this corrected Lagrangian, not the one as currently printed. This is a minor typographical issue, but correcting the exponent (and, if desired, harmonizing the underline notation for $\hat b_\ell$) would remove the internal inconsistency in this display.

comments.title: Unclear notation in proof of Proposition 2
comments.paragraph:
Cosine similarity. We now turn to the cosine similarity result. We focus on the case of strategic complements. The proof for the case of strategic substitutes is analogous. We start by writing a useful explicit expression for $\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)$:

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|\left\|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right\|}=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\sqrt{C}},
$$

where the last equality follows because, at the optimum, $\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}=C$. ... Hence, using this in equation (12), we can deduce that

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{b}_{1} \geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon} \quad \text { iff } \quad\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C(1-\epsilon) \geq 0 .
$$
comments.quote:
Cosine similarity. We now turn to the cosine similarity result. We focus on the case of strategic complements. The proof for the case of strategic substitutes is analogous. We start by writing a useful explicit expression for $\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)$:

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|\left\|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right\|}=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\sqrt{C}},
$$

where the last equality follows because, at the optimum, $\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}=C$. ... Hence, using this in equation (12), we can deduce that

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{b}_{1} \geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon} \quad \text { iff } \quad\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C(1-\epsilon) \geq 0 .
$$
comments.message:
At first the cosine‑similarity part of the proof of Proposition 2 was a bit hard to parse because of notation. The vector $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*$ is not explicitly defined, and the text switches between $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)$ and $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)$. From the displayed equality
$$
\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)
= \frac{(\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}})\cdot(\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)}
{\|\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|\,\|\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1\|},
$$
one can infer that $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*=\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$, and using $\|\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2=C$ and $\|\boldsymbol{u}^1\|=1$ it is easy to verify that $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)=\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)$. Still, defining $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*$ explicitly (or sticking to $\boldsymbol{y}^*$) and noting the scale‑invariance of cosine similarity would make this part of the proof smoother to follow.

zero_shot.label: Zero Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a well-written paper that makes a clear methodological contribution by applying principal component decomposition to analyze optimal interventions in network games. The mathematical framework is carefully developed, and the main results are intuitive and well-explained. However, there are a few technical issues worth noting: some definitions could be more precise, there is a potential inconsistency in how the cost function is specified across different parts of the paper, and some derivations skip steps that would benefit from additional justification. The paper generally maintains consistency between its formal results and prose descriptions, though a few minor clarifications would strengthen the presentation.

comments.title: Potential error in cosine similarity definition
comments.quote: DEFINITION 1: The cosine similarity of two nonzero vectors $\boldsymbol{y}$ and $\boldsymbol{z}$ is $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\|}$.
comments.explanation: The standard definition of cosine similarity is the dot product divided by the product of both norms: $\frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\| \|\boldsymbol{z}\|}$. The definition given here is missing $\|\boldsymbol{z}\|$ in the denominator. Looking at how this is used later in the paper (e.g., in the proof of Proposition 2, equation (12), where they write $\rho(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1})=\frac{(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot (\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1})}{\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|\|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\|}$), the authors clearly intend the standard definition with both norms. This appears to be a typographical error in Definition 1.

comments.title: Ambiguity in sign convention for eigenvector uniqueness
comments.quote: For generic $\boldsymbol{G}$, the decomposition is uniquely determined, except that any column of $\boldsymbol{U}$ is determined only up to multiplication by -1.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that eigenvectors are determined only up to sign. However, this creates potential ambiguity in interpreting results like Proposition 1 part 2a, which states that $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})) \rightarrow 1$. Since $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$ is only determined up to sign, one might worry whether this should be $|\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G}))| \rightarrow 1$. Looking at the context, particularly the Definition 2 of simple interventions which also uses $u_i^1$ without specifying sign, and the fact that the Perron-Frobenius theorem guarantees $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$ can be chosen to have all positive entries (mentioned later in the discussion of eigenvector centrality), the authors likely intend to fix this positive sign convention for $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$. However, this convention should be stated explicitly to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Inconsistency between welfare formula and stated Property A
comments.quote: Property A: The aggregate equilibrium utility is proportional to the sum of the squares of the equilibrium actions, that is, $W(\boldsymbol{b}, \boldsymbol{G})=w \cdot\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{*}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{a}^{*}$ for some $w \in \mathbb{R}$, where $\boldsymbol{a}^{*}$ is the Nash equilibrium action profile.
comments.explanation: In Example 2 (Local Public Goods), the authors state that the aggregate equilibrium utility is $W(\boldsymbol{b}, \boldsymbol{G})=-\left(\boldsymbol{a}^{*}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{a}^{*}$, corresponding to $w=-1$. However, I traced through the calculation: with $U_i(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{G})=-\frac{1}{2}(\tau-x_i)^2-\frac{1}{2}a_i^2$, summing over all agents and substituting the equilibrium condition would give a more complex expression involving cross-terms from $(\tau-x_i)^2 = (\tau - \tilde{b}_i - a_i - \tilde{\beta}\sum_j g_{ij}a_j)^2$. The claim that this simplifies to $-\boldsymbol{a}^{\top}\boldsymbol{a}$ after the change of variables is stated without derivation. While this may be correct, the verification is non-trivial and would benefit from explicit calculation or a reference to where it is shown in the supplementary material.

comments.title: Missing justification for equilibrium uniqueness under the transformation
comments.quote: Performing the change of variables $b_{i}=\left[\tau-b_{i}\right] / 2$ and $\beta=-\tilde{\beta} / 2$ (with the status quo equal to $\hat{b}_{i}=\left[\tau-\tilde{b}_{i}\right] / 2$) yields a best-response structure exactly as in condition (2).
comments.explanation: The change of variables appears to have a typo: $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$ should likely be $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$ (using the tilde notation from the local public goods setup). More substantively, after this change of variables, the authors should verify that Assumption 2 (spectral radius condition) still holds with the new $\beta = -\tilde{\beta}/2$ to ensure equilibrium uniqueness. Since $\tilde{\beta} \in (0,1)$, we have $|\beta| = \tilde{\beta}/2 < 1/2$, which would satisfy the spectral radius condition if $\boldsymbol{G}$ has spectral radius at most 2, but this is not explicitly verified.

comments.title: Incomplete specification of when the budget constraint binds
comments.quote: First, at the optimal solution $\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, the resource constraint problem must bind.
comments.explanation: In the proof of Theorem 1, the authors argue that the constraint must bind at the optimum. The argument for the case $w > 0$ is clear: if the constraint doesn't bind, we can increase some $x_\ell$ to increase the objective. However, for $w < 0$, the argument is less clear as stated. They write 'If $w>0$ (resp. $w<0$), the increase or the decrease is guaranteed to increase (resp. decrease) the corresponding $(x_\ell+1)^2$.' For $w < 0$, we want to minimize $\sum_\ell w\alpha_\ell(1+x_\ell)^2\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$, which means minimizing $(1+x_\ell)^2$. But the constraint limits how negative $x_\ell$ can be. The argument that the constraint binds when $w < 0$ and $\sum_\ell \underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2 > C$ (from Assumption 3) needs more careful justification - it relies on showing that the optimal $x_\ell$ values require using the full budget.

comments.title: Potential sign issue in the similarity ratio definition and Corollary 1
comments.quote: Corollary 1: Suppose Assumptions 1-3 hold and the network game satisfies Property A. If the game is one of strategic complements ( $\beta>0$ ), then $\left|r_{\ell}^{*}\right|$ is decreasing in $\ell$; if the game is one of strategic substitutes ( $\beta<0$ ), then $\left|r_{\ell}^{*}\right|$ is increasing in $\ell$.
comments.explanation: The similarity ratio is defined as $r_\ell^* = \rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*, \boldsymbol{u}^\ell(\boldsymbol{G}))/\rho(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^\ell(\boldsymbol{G}))$. From Theorem 1, this ratio is proportional to $\frac{w\alpha_\ell}{\mu - w\alpha_\ell}$. For strategic complements ($\beta > 0$) with $w > 0$, we have $\alpha_\ell$ decreasing in $\ell$ and $\mu > w\alpha_1 > w\alpha_\ell$, so $\frac{w\alpha_\ell}{\mu - w\alpha_\ell}$ is indeed positive and decreasing in $\ell$. However, the corollary states the result for $|r_\ell^*|$, suggesting the authors want to cover cases where the ratio might be negative. Looking at the formula, if $w < 0$ (which is allowed by Property A), then $\frac{w\alpha_\ell}{\mu - w\alpha_\ell}$ could have different sign behavior. The proof in the appendix doesn't explicitly address the case $w < 0$ for this corollary, though the general framework should apply.

rag_local.label: RAG Local
rag_local.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a technically sophisticated and intellectually compelling approach to the problem of optimal network interventions, leveraging spectral decomposition to characterize how planners should target individuals based on whether strategic interactions exhibit complements or substitutes. The core insight—that optimal interventions align with top eigenvectors under complements and bottom eigenvectors under substitutes—is elegant and economically intuitive, connecting global versus local network structure to the nature of spillover effects. The writing in the introduction is clear and effectively motivates the problem. However, several concerns merit attention: First, the practical applicability remains somewhat unclear, as real-world planners rarely know the full network structure or interaction matrix with sufficient precision to compute eigenvectors, and the paper would benefit from discussing robustness to network uncertainty or estimation error. Second, the assumption of a utilitarian welfare criterion and separable intervention costs, while tractable, may limit relevance for settings where equity concerns or fixed costs of reaching individuals matter. Third, the result that "large budget" interventions become simple (proportional to a single principal component) raises questions about what constitutes "large" in empirically relevant cases and whether the convergence is fast enough to provide useful guidance. Finally, the abstract and introduction could better distinguish this contribution from existing work on network games and Bonacich centrality-based targeting to sharpen the paper's marginal contribution.

comments.title: Orthogonality claim requires symmetric interaction matrix
comments.quote: (c) the principal components are orthogonal, so the effects along various principal components can be treated separately.
comments.explanation: The claim that principal components (eigenvectors) are orthogonal is only guaranteed when the interaction matrix is symmetric. In network games, the interaction matrix G often represents directed relationships where g_ij ≠ g_ji, meaning one agent's effect on another need not be reciprocated equally. For asymmetric matrices, eigenvectors are generally not orthogonal, and one would need to work with left and right eigenvectors separately. The paper's context in para 4 describes interactions where 'the strength of a link between two individuals reflects how strongly the action of one affects the marginal returns experienced by the other,' which does not explicitly require symmetry. While this is an introductory section and the formal model likely specifies symmetry assumptions, the passage states this orthogonality as a general property without qualification. A careful reader working with directed networks could be misled into thinking the decomposition approach applies directly to their asymmetric setting.

comments.title: Missing boldface on vectors in equilibrium equation
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, vectors are consistently denoted with boldface (e.g., $\boldsymbol{a}$, $\boldsymbol{b}$, $\boldsymbol{G}$ in para 15-16). However, in this equation, $a^{*}$, $b$, $I$, and $G$ appear without boldface. Comparing to para 16 where $\boldsymbol{a}$, $\boldsymbol{G}$, and $\boldsymbol{b}$ are all bolded, this represents a notation inconsistency. While a reader familiar with the context would understand these are vectors/matrices, the inconsistent notation could momentarily confuse readers about whether scalar or vector quantities are intended. This is a minor notational inconsistency rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Mismatched footnote marker
comments.explanation: I noticed that Assumption 2 is preceded by '[^3]' which appears to be a footnote marker, but Assumption 1 uses a superscript '7' for its footnote. This creates an inconsistency in notation. Looking at the passage, Assumption 1 has footnote 7, so one would expect Assumption 2's footnote to be footnote 8 (which is indeed referenced later in the sentence with the superscript 8). The '[^3]' marker appears to be a formatting artifact or error that doesn't match the paper's footnote numbering scheme. While this is partly a formatting issue, it creates genuine ambiguity about what footnote 3 refers to and whether there's missing content.

comments.title: Incomplete justification for 'distinct eigenvalues holds generically'
comments.quote: all eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{G}$ are distinct. (The latter condition holds generically.)
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the condition of distinct eigenvalues 'holds generically' for symmetric matrices. While this is indeed a standard result in linear algebra (symmetric matrices with repeated eigenvalues form a measure-zero set in the space of symmetric matrices), the claim deserves more careful qualification. For arbitrary symmetric matrices this is true, but network adjacency matrices are constrained: they have non-negative entries, zero diagonal (as stated in para 17), and often additional structure (e.g., binary entries for unweighted networks). The generic property for unconstrained symmetric matrices doesn't automatically transfer to these constrained subspaces. A careful reader working with specific network classes (e.g., regular graphs, which often have repeated eigenvalues) might be misled into thinking this assumption is innocuous when it may actually be restrictive for their application.

comments.title: Overstated claim about necessity and sufficiency
comments.quote: Assumption 2 ensures that (2) is a necessary and sufficient condition for each individual to be best-responding
comments.explanation: Initially, I was puzzled by this claim. Equation (2) in para 18 is simply the first-order condition derived from maximizing the quadratic utility function. For a quadratic objective that is concave in the own action (which it is, given the -½a_i² term), the FOC is always necessary and sufficient for a maximum—this follows from basic convex optimization and does not depend on Assumption 2 (the spectral radius condition). Assumption 2 is needed for the *existence of a unique equilibrium* and for the *invertibility* of [I - βG], but not for the FOC to characterize individual best responses. The authors conflate the role of Assumption 2 in ensuring equilibrium uniqueness/existence with the separate question of whether the FOC characterizes best responses. The latter holds regardless of the spectral radius condition, as long as the utility is strictly concave in own action (which it always is here).

comments.title: Insufficient justification for equilibrium utility formula
comments.explanation: Starting from the utility function in para 31, $U_i = a_i(b_i + \beta \sum_j g_{ij} a_j) - \frac{1}{2}a_i^2$, I attempted to verify this claim. At an interior equilibrium, the first-order condition gives $b_i + \beta \sum_j g_{ij} a_j^* = a_i^*$. Substituting this back into the utility function yields $U_i = a_i^* \cdot a_i^* - \frac{1}{2}(a_i^*)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(a_i^*)^2$. So the formula is indeed correct. However, the authors say only 'It can be verified' without indicating the key step (using the FOC to substitute). While this is a standard technique in the network games literature, the non-obvious insight that equilibrium utility takes such a simple quadratic form—independent of $\beta$ and the network structure except through $a_i^*$—arguably warrants at least a brief indication of the derivation method, especially since this result drives subsequent welfare analysis.

comments.title: Typographical error in variable definition creates circular reference
comments.explanation: Initially I noticed this formula defines b_i in terms of itself, which is mathematically meaningless. Looking at the context, para 37 introduces the base level as $\tilde{b}_i$, and para 40 later correctly writes the status quo as $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$. The pattern suggests the intended definition should be $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$, using the tilde-decorated variable from the original model. This appears to be a typographical error where $\tilde{b}_i$ was accidentally written as $b_i$ on the right-hand side.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the change of variables yielding best-response structure
comments.quote: Performing the change of variables $b_{i}=\left[\tau-b_{i}\right] / 2$ and $\beta=-\tilde{\beta} / 2$ (with the status quo equal to $\hat{b}_{i}=\left[\tau-\tilde{b}_{i}\right] / 2$) yields a best-response structure exactly as in condition (2).
comments.explanation: The authors claim this change of variables yields the best-response structure in condition (2), but the derivation is non-trivial and completely omitted. Starting from the utility function in para 38, one must derive the first-order conditions, substitute the expression for $x_i$ from para 37, and then show how the proposed variable transformation maps this to the canonical form. The transformation $\beta = -\tilde{\beta}/2$ with $\tilde{\beta} > 0$ gives $\beta < 0$, which is consistent with strategic substitutes. However, the factor of 1/2 in both transformations and how they interact with the quadratic cost terms requires verification. A reader attempting to reproduce this would need substantial additional work to confirm the claim.

comments.title: Aggregate welfare formula stated without derivation
comments.explanation: This welfare expression is stated as a result but not derived. From the utility function in para 38, summing over agents gives $W = \sum_i U_i = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_i (\tau - x_i)^2 - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i a_i^2$. It is not obvious how this simplifies to $-(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$ at equilibrium. This would require using the first-order conditions and the network structure in a specific way. While para 41 notes this satisfies Property A (welfare proportional to sum of squared actions), the specific coefficient of -1 (rather than some other constant) requires justification that isn't provided.

comments.title: Missing justification for norm equality in correlation formula
comments.explanation: The first line defines ρ as the dot product divided by the product of both norms: ||y*|| and ||u^ℓ(G)||. The second line shows only √C in the denominator, which implicitly assumes both ||y*|| = √C and ||u^ℓ(G)|| = 1. While eigenvectors are conventionally normalized to unit length, this is not stated. More importantly, the claim ||y*|| = √C appears to come from a constraint of the form ||y*||² = C or Σ_ℓ (y_ℓ*)² = C, but this constraint is not shown in the provided context. Paragraph 150 shows a constraint involving w²b̂_ℓ² terms, not simply (y_ℓ*)². The derivation skips explaining how the optimization constraint translates to ||y*|| = √C, which is a non-trivial step that readers would need to verify the correlation formula.

comments.title: Unexplained relationship between b̂_ℓ and correlation with eigenvector
comments.explanation: This step claims b̂_ℓ = ||b̂|| · ρ(b̂, u^ℓ(G)). For this to hold, we need b̂_ℓ to be the ℓ-th component of b̂ in the eigenvector basis, AND we need u^ℓ(G) to be a standard basis vector in that representation (so that ρ(b̂, u^ℓ(G)) = b̂_ℓ/||b̂||). The notation suggests b̂_ℓ is an eigenspace coefficient and u^ℓ(G) is the ℓ-th eigenvector of G. If we're working in the eigenbasis, then b̂ · u^ℓ = b̂_ℓ (when u^ℓ is unit), giving ρ(b̂, u^ℓ) = b̂_ℓ/||b̂||, which would make the equality correct. However, this interpretation requires understanding the coordinate system, which is not made explicit in the passage and could confuse readers about what space the correlation is computed in.

comments.title: Missing justification for why $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) \rightarrow 0$ when $x_{\ell}^{*}$ converges to a finite limit
comments.quote: This implies that $x_{\ell}^{*} \rightarrow \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$ for all $\ell \neq 1$. As a result, if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow$ 0 for all $\ell \neq 1$.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) \rightarrow 0$ 'as a result' of $x_{\ell}^{*}$ converging to a finite limit $\frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$. However, from para 155, we have $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) = \frac{\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|}{\sqrt{C}} \rho(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) x_{\ell}^{*}$. For this to go to zero as $C \rightarrow \infty$, we need the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}}$ factor to dominate, which requires that $x_{\ell}^{*}$ remains bounded. This is indeed the case for $\ell \neq 1$ since $x_{\ell}^{*}$ converges to a finite value. But the stated reasoning ('as a result') suggests the conclusion follows directly from the limit of $x_{\ell}^{*}$, when actually it follows from the combination of bounded $x_{\ell}^{*}$ and the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}}$ factor. The key step that $\frac{\text{bounded}}{\sqrt{C}} \rightarrow 0$ is skipped, making the derivation appear incomplete.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: double-hat on $\hat{\hat{b}}_\ell$ versus single-hat elsewhere
comments.explanation: Looking at this definition of D, I notice it uses $\hat{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ (double-hat) in the summation. However, comparing with para 162, the expression for $W^s$ uses $\hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ (underline with single hat). The quantity D is explicitly stated to equal $W^s$ based on the context (since $W^s = \underline{\hat{b}}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} \tilde{x}_{1}(\tilde{x}_{1}+2)+\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$). The double-hat notation $\hat{\hat{b}}$ appears nowhere else in the provided context and seems to be a typographical error for $\underline{\hat{b}}$ or simply $\hat{b}$. This inconsistency could confuse readers trying to follow the proof.

comments.title: Typo in coefficient expression: $\alpha_2^*$ instead of $\alpha_2$
comments.explanation: Looking at this line, I see $\alpha_2^*$ appearing in the expression $(2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2^*)$. However, checking the supporting calculation in para 167, the derived bound is $(\frac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1-\alpha_2})^2(2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\|\hat{b}\|^2$, which has $\alpha_2$ without any asterisk. The asterisk notation $\alpha_2^*$ is not defined anywhere in the context and appears to be a typographical error. The next line simplifies this to $2\|\hat{b}\|^2/C \cdot (\alpha_2/(\alpha_1-\alpha_2))^2$, which is consistent with using $(2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)/\alpha_1 \leq 2$, confirming the intended expression should have $\alpha_2$ not $\alpha_2^*$.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: $\hat{b}_\ell$ vs $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$
comments.explanation: In the denominator, the passage uses $\hat{b}_1^2$ (without underline), but para 161 defines the simple intervention using $\underline{\hat{b}}_1$ (with underline), stating $\tilde{x}_1 = \sqrt{C}/\underline{\hat{b}}_1$. Para 166 also refers to $\underline{b}_1^2 \tilde{x}_1^2 = C$. The relationship $\hat{b}_1^2 \tilde{x}_1^2 = C$ would only hold if $\hat{b}_1 = \underline{\hat{b}}_1$, but the paper appears to distinguish these notations (underlined vs non-underlined versions appear in different places). If these are meant to be the same quantity, the notation should be consistent; if they differ, the equality used in the derivation may not hold.

comments.title: First inequality step lacks explicit justification
comments.explanation: The step from the first line to claiming the first fraction is $\leq 1$ requires showing that $\hat{b}_1^2 \alpha_1 x_1^*(x_1^*+2) + \sum_{\ell=1}^n \alpha_\ell \hat{b}_\ell^2 \leq D = \underline{\hat{b}}_1^2 \alpha_1 \tilde{x}_1(\tilde{x}_1+2) + \sum_{\ell=1}^n \alpha_\ell \underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$. This is non-trivial because the optimal intervention $x^*$ and simple intervention $\tilde{x}$ are different policies with potentially different $\hat{b}$ values. The authors don't explain why the numerator is bounded by $D$. This likely follows from optimality properties or budget constraints, but the justification is missing for a key step.

comments.title: Incorrect simplification in cosine similarity formula
comments.explanation: The cosine similarity formula appears to have an error in simplification. Starting from the standard definition, the numerator is $(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot (\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}) = \sqrt{C}(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1}$. The denominator is $\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| \cdot \|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\| = \sqrt{C} \cdot \sqrt{C} \cdot 1 = C$, using the stated fact that $\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = C$ (so $\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| = \sqrt{C}$) and that $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$ is a unit vector. Thus the ratio should be $\frac{\sqrt{C}(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1}}{C} = \frac{(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1}}{\sqrt{C}}$. However, cosine similarity is dimensionless and scale-invariant, so the left-hand side should simply equal $(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1} / \|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| = (\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1} / \sqrt{C}$. While the final expression is numerically correct, this only works because $\boldsymbol{u}^1$ is a unit vector. The issue is that the $\sqrt{C}$ in $\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1$ should cancel completely in a proper cosine similarity calculation (since cosine similarity is scale-invariant), making the appearance of $\sqrt{C}$ in the final denominator potentially confusing or suggesting a non-standard interpretation.

comments.title: Missing factor in intermediate summation step
comments.explanation: Looking at para 175, the authors write $b_{i}^{*}-\hat{b}_{i}=w \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} u_{\ell}^{i} \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}} \hat{b}_{\ell}$, where the sum involves $\hat{b}_{\ell}$ (standard coordinates). However, in para 176, when computing the dot product $(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1}$, the summation suddenly uses $\hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}$ (the rotated/eigencoordinate version, as defined in para 174 where $\underline{\boldsymbol{b}}=\boldsymbol{U}^{T} \boldsymbol{b}$). This substitution appears without justification. The derivation seems to conflate $\hat{b}_{\ell}$ with $\hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}$. While the final answer may be correct if the authors meant to use eigencoordinates throughout, the intermediate step as written is inconsistent with the expression in para 175.

comments.title: Index notation inconsistency between para 175 and para 176
comments.explanation: In para 175, the eigenvector component is written as $u_{\ell}^{i}$ (subscript $\ell$, superscript $i$), but in para 176, the same type of component appears as $u_{i}^{1}$ and $u_{i}^{\ell}$ (subscript $i$, superscript $1$ or $\ell$). The convention seems to be that superscript denotes which eigenvector and subscript denotes which component, so $u_{i}^{\ell}$ means the $i$-th component of eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}$. Under this reading, para 175 should have $u_{i}^{\ell}$ not $u_{\ell}^{i}$. This notational inconsistency could confuse readers trying to verify the derivation.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: $\hat{b}_{1}$ vs $\hat{\underline{b}}_{1}$
comments.explanation: In paragraph 176, the authors correctly derive that $(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1} = \frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{\underline{b}}_{1}$, where $\hat{\underline{b}}_{1}$ denotes the first component of $\underline{\boldsymbol{b}} = \boldsymbol{U}^{T} \boldsymbol{b}$ (i.e., the projection onto the eigenvector basis). However, in paragraph 177, this becomes $\hat{b}_{1}$ without the underline. The notation established in paragraph 174 clearly distinguishes $\boldsymbol{b}$ (original coordinates) from $\underline{\boldsymbol{b}}$ (eigenvector coordinates). Unless $\hat{b}_{1}$ is intended to mean the same as $\hat{\underline{b}}_{1}$, this is a notational inconsistency that could confuse readers about which coordinate system is being used.

comments.title: Missing absolute value for correlation inequality
comments.explanation: The expression $\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{b}_{1}$ could be negative if $\hat{b}_{1} < 0$ (or potentially if $\mu < w\alpha_1$, depending on the optimization setup). A correlation $\rho$ being $\geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon}$ typically requires considering alignment in either direction, so one would expect $|\rho| \geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon}$ or the authors should ensure positivity. The 'iff' equivalence on the right-hand side involves a squared term, which handles signs correctly, but the left-hand inequality as written presumes the expression is non-negative. This could be intentional if the authors' setup guarantees $\hat{b}_1 > 0$, but this assumption is not stated in the provided context.

comments.title: Unexplained equality relating C to the sum involving binding budget constraint
comments.explanation: The authors substitute C with the sum $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}$, claiming this follows from 'the condition on the binding budget constraint at the optimum' from Theorem 1. However, this substitution is non-trivial and the specific form of the binding budget constraint that yields this exact equality is not shown in the provided context. A reader cannot verify this step without seeing Theorem 1. The derivation would benefit from either showing the relevant equation from Theorem 1 or providing a brief explanation of how the budget constraint implies $C = \sum_{\ell=1}^{n}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}$.

comments.title: Unclear notation change from $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}$ to $\|\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}\|^{2}$
comments.quote: The next equality follows by noticing that $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}=\|\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}\|^{2}$.
comments.explanation: In para 185, the explanation states that $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}=\|\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}\|^{2}$, but the notation $\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}$ does not appear anywhere in the passage being checked or the surrounding context. The actual equality being justified in the chain is expanding $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}$ as $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}$, which is a standard definition of the squared norm. The reference to $\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}$ appears to be either a typo or references notation defined elsewhere that may confuse readers who don't have access to that definition.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the interpretation of optimal intervention outcome
comments.quote: the optimal intervention in that case is to modify the endowment of each individual so that everyone accesses the optimal level of the local public good without investing
comments.explanation: The footnote states that when $C \geq\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|$ and $w=-1$, we have $b_i^* = 0$. Using the change of variables $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$, this implies $\tilde{b}_i = \tau$ for all $i$. The claim is that this means 'everyone accesses the optimal level of the local public good without investing.' However, the passage does not clarify what 'without investing' means in this context—does it mean agents choose zero action ($a_i = 0$), or that no policy intervention is needed beyond setting endowments? If $b_i^* = 0$ refers to the intervention variable being zero, this seems to contradict the statement that endowments are being modified. Without seeing Example 2's full setup, a careful reader could be confused about whether $b_i^* = 0$ means no intervention is applied or whether it means the transformed intervention variable equals zero while actual endowment modifications occur. The interpretation hinges on the precise definitions in Example 2, which are not provided in context, but the phrasing could mislead readers about what $b_i^* = 0$ actually implies for policy.

comments.title: Unverified monotonicity claim for ratio of consecutive principal component weights
comments.quote: It can be verified that, for every $\ell \in\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$, the ratio $x_{\ell} / x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing (decreasing) in $\beta$ for the case of strategic complements (substitutes)
comments.explanation: The authors claim a monotonicity result about how the ratio of consecutive principal component weights changes with β. However, the passage provides no definition of what x_ℓ represents (presumably weights on principal components from optimization), and the claim is stated as 'can be verified' without proof or reference. The result is non-trivial: it requires showing that for ALL consecutive pairs ℓ and ℓ+1, the ratio behaves monotonically in β, which depends on the specific structure of how eigenvalues enter the optimal solution. While footnote 14 mentions that βΛ has entries less than 1 (from Assumption 2), this alone doesn't establish the claimed monotonicity. The claim may well be true, but given that this is a footnote making a mathematical assertion affecting interpretation of the main results, the justification is insufficient for a reader to verify the claim without substantial additional work.

comments.title: Claim about ratio behavior appears to conflate two different quantities
comments.quote: we have that $\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}$ and $r_{\ell}^{*} / r_{\ell^{\prime}}^{*}$ are both decreasing in $C$
comments.explanation: The footnote claims two quantities are 'both decreasing in C'. However, the expression $\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}$ does not obviously depend on $C$ at all—it appears to be a function of model parameters ($w$, $\alpha_\ell$, $\mu$) but not the budget constraint $C$. This raises a concern: either (1) $\mu$ implicitly depends on $C$ in a way not stated here, (2) the authors meant a different expression that does depend on $C$, or (3) there is an error in the claim. The parallel footnote 16 in para 203 discusses how the ratio $x_\ell/x_{\ell+1}$ changes with $\beta$, which is a different type of comparative static. Without seeing the main text this footnote refers to, it's difficult to fully resolve, but as written, claiming a $C$-independent expression is 'decreasing in $C$' is technically incorrect or at minimum requires clarification about which parameters depend on the budget.

comments.title: Inconsistent vector reference in decomposition claim
comments.quote: Recall that $\left\|\frac{1}{n} \hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}$ is equal to the sum of $\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2}$ (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$ ) and the sum of squared deviations of the entries of the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ from their mean.
comments.explanation: The passage describes the squared mean as being 'of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$' but the formula clearly involves $\hat{b}_i$ (with hat). The rest of the passage correctly uses $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$, so this appears to be a typo where $\boldsymbol{b}$ should read $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$. This inconsistency could confuse readers about which vector is being discussed, especially since $\boldsymbol{b}$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ likely have distinct meanings in this paper.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for squared norm decomposition
comments.quote: Recall that $\left\|\frac{1}{n} \hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}$ is equal to the sum of $\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2}$ (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$ ) and the sum of squared deviations of the entries of the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ from their mean.
comments.explanation: The claimed identity appears mathematically incorrect. We have $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2$. The standard variance decomposition states $\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2 = n\bar{b}^2 + \sum_i(\hat{b}_i - \bar{b})^2$ where $\bar{b} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i$. Thus $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = \frac{1}{n}\bar{b}^2 + \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i(\hat{b}_i - \bar{b})^2$. The first term equals $\frac{1}{n}(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i)^2$, not $(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i)^2$, and the second term has a $\frac{1}{n^2}$ factor. The identity as stated would require different scaling. Perhaps the authors intended to state something about $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ or $\frac{1}{n}\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ instead.

comments.title: Variance-covariance matrix formula may need clarification on centering
comments.quote: When we look at the variance-covariance matrix of $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}$ defined by $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}}=\boldsymbol{O}(\boldsymbol{b}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}})$, the variance-covariance matrix becomes $\boldsymbol{O} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \boldsymbol{O}^{\top}$
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the notation here. The authors define the transformation as $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}}=\boldsymbol{O}(\boldsymbol{b}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}})$, which implies $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}} = \overline{\boldsymbol{b}} + \boldsymbol{O}(\boldsymbol{b}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}})$. If $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is the variance-covariance matrix of $\boldsymbol{b}$, then the variance-covariance matrix of $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}$ would indeed be $\boldsymbol{O}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\boldsymbol{O}^{\top}$ since adding a constant $\overline{\boldsymbol{b}}$ doesn't affect covariance. The claim that $\boldsymbol{O}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\boldsymbol{O}^{\top}$ has the same eigenvalues as $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is correct if $\boldsymbol{O}$ is orthogonal (since similar matrices have the same eigenvalues, and $\boldsymbol{O}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\boldsymbol{O}^{\top}$ is similar to $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ via the orthogonal transformation). However, the passage doesn't explicitly state that $\boldsymbol{O}$ is an orthogonal matrix, which is essential for this conclusion. A reader unfamiliar with the notation conventions might not know this assumption is being made.

comments.title: Incomplete characterization of Assumption 3 negation
comments.quote: if Assumption 3 does not hold (i.e., $w<0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_\ell^{2} \leq C$ )
comments.explanation: The footnote states that Assumption 3 not holding is equivalent to '$w<0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_\ell^{2} \leq C$'. However, the logical negation of a condition typically involves more cases. If Assumption 3 were stated as '$w \geq 0$ or $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_\ell^{2} > C$', then its negation would indeed be '$w < 0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_\ell^{2} \leq C$'. Without seeing Assumption 3 explicitly, I cannot verify this is correct, but the phrasing 'i.e.' suggests an equivalence that may be incomplete or may mischaracterize what Assumption 3 actually states. Additionally, the claim that $x_\ell^* = -1$ for all $\ell$ is optimal in this case is stated without justification—it's not immediately obvious why this corner solution would be optimal when the assumption fails, as opposed to some other boundary or interior solution.

## High-Level Feedback

This paper tackles an important and well-motivated problem—optimal targeting of interventions in network games—and develops an elegant analytical framework based on spectral decomposition that yields clean, interpretable results. The core insight connecting strategic complements/substitutes to the relevance of global versus local network structure (high versus low eigenvalue principal components) is both novel and economically intuitive. The writing is generally clear, and the progressive structure from the main characterization theorem through corollaries on strategic interaction types to results on "simple" interventions is logically organized. However, several issues merit attention. First, the practical applicability of the results deserves more discussion: eigenvector-based interventions require complete knowledge of the network structure, and the paper should address robustness to network measurement error or partial observability. Second, the assumption of a utilitarian planner with separable intervention costs, while tractable, may not capture realistic policy constraints such as discrete targeting decisions or equity considerations—acknowledging these limitations would strengthen the contribution. Third, the connection to empirical work is underdeveloped; even a brief discussion of how these theoretical predictions might be tested or calibrated would enhance the paper's impact. Finally, while the large-budget asymptotic results (Propositions 1-2) are elegant, more guidance on what constitutes "large enough" in practical terms would help readers assess when the simplified recommendations apply.

comments.title: Typo in change of variables for local public goods example
comments.explanation: The change of variables formula uses $b_i$ on both sides of the equation, which is mathematically ill-defined. Based on the context, where $\tilde{b}_i$ is the base level of public good in the original formulation, the intended transformation should be $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$. This is consistent with the immediately following statement that the status quo is $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$. The authors clearly intend to transform from the original parameter $\tilde{b}_i$ to the new parameter $b_i$, but the formula as written contains a notational error.

comments.title: Missing normalization term in cosine similarity definition
comments.quote: DEFINITION 1: The cosine similarity of two nonzero vectors $\boldsymbol{y}$ and $\boldsymbol{z}$ is $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\|}$.
comments.explanation: The standard definition of cosine similarity is the dot product divided by the product of both norms: $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z}) = \frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\| \|\boldsymbol{z}\|}$. The definition given only divides by $\|\boldsymbol{y}\|$, missing $\|\boldsymbol{z}\|$ in the denominator. While the authors apply this to unit eigenvectors where $\|\boldsymbol{z}\| = 1$, the definition as stated is incomplete—it only works correctly when the second argument is a unit vector, which should be noted explicitly.

comments.title: Eigenvector stated as maximizer instead of minimizer for bottom eigenvalues
comments.quote: Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second
comments.explanation: The text states that $\lambda_n$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$ are defined as minimization problems (using 'min'), but then claims that $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ and $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ are 'maximizers' of these problems. This is a direct contradiction. If $\lambda_n = \min_{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1} \sum_{i,j} g_{ij} u_i u_j$, then $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ should be a minimizer, not a maximizer. This appears to be a copy-paste error where 'maximizer' was not changed to 'minimizer' when adapting the text.

comments.title: Missing index in cost function formula
comments.explanation: The summation is written as $\sum_{\in \mathcal{N}}$ which is missing the index variable. It should read $\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}}$ to be consistent with the notation used throughout the paper and to properly define the sum over individual variances $\sigma_{ii}^{\mathcal{B}}$. This appears to be a typographical error where the index 'i' was dropped.

comments.title: Missing squared factor in Lagrangian term
comments.explanation: In the objective function of the optimization problem (IT-PC) shown just above, the term is $\alpha_{\ell}(1+x_{\ell})^{2} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ with $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$ squared. However, in the Lagrangian, the first sum has $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$ without the square. This appears to be a typographical error. The subsequent first-order condition correctly shows $2 \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}[...]$ which is consistent with the squared version, suggesting the Lagrangian should have $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation between underlined and non-underlined b in constraint
comments.explanation: The optimization problem uses $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ (with underline indicating projection onto principal components) in the constraint, but the Lagrangian and FOC use $\hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}$ without the underline. According to the paper's notation, $\underline{\boldsymbol{z}} = \boldsymbol{U}^\top \boldsymbol{z}$ denotes the projection, so $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$ and $\hat{b}_{\ell}$ are different quantities. The FOC should use $\underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$ to be consistent with the stated problem.

comments.title: Typo in equilibrium condition: matrix β instead of scalar
comments.explanation: The paper consistently defines $\beta$ as a scalar parameter capturing strategic interdependencies. However, here it is written in bold as $\boldsymbol{\beta}$, suggesting a matrix or vector. The correct expression should be $[\boldsymbol{I}-\beta \boldsymbol{\Lambda}]^{-1}$ with scalar $\beta$, as written elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: Summation notation error with superscript
comments.explanation: The summation is written as '$\sum \ell=1^{n}$' which appears to be a rendering error. It should be '$\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}$' with the index as a subscript.

comments.title: Typographical error in factor within inequality chain
comments.explanation: The term $\alpha_2^*$ appears in the numerator, but there is no definition of $\alpha_2^*$ anywhere in the paper. Looking at the subsequent line which shows $2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2$ (without the asterisk), it appears $\alpha_2^*$ should simply be $\alpha_2$. This is likely a typographical error.

comments.title: Index notation error in intermediate expression
comments.explanation: Based on the paper's notation where $u_i^\ell$ denotes the $i$th entry of the $\ell$th eigenvector, the notation $u_\ell^i$ reverses the convention. Additionally, the expression uses $\hat{b}_\ell$ instead of $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$ for the principal component projections. The correct expression should use $u_i^\ell$ and $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$ for consistent notation.
